facts.text: Patchogue was founded in 1756 during the French and Indian War by a lottery, after the land was deeded to John Winthrop Jr. in 1664.
The village incorporated as an official municipality in 1893, cementing its status on Long Island’s South Shore.
Three Patchogue churches—Congregational, United Methodist, and St. —are listed on the National Register of Historic Places, highlighting the town’s historic religious architecture.
The Patchogue‑Medford Library showcases a mural titled “Scenes Along the Patchogue River,” painted by local artist Zoeller to celebrate the town’s waterfront.
Patchogue’s landscape is unusually flat because glaciers never advanced that far south, leaving the area free of moraines.
Patchogue’s LIRR station is a major transit hub, with eight Suffolk County bus routes converging there for commuters.
